mcqs.explanation: When catching by reference, the first matching catch block is executed. Since Base is the first catch block and Derived is a subclass of Base, the Base catch block executes due to polymorphism. Derived catch block is unreachable.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is the correct way to catch exceptions in a class hierarchy?
mcqs.explanation: In exception handling, catch blocks must be ordered from most derived (specific) to least derived (general) class. Catching Derived first ensures proper handling before falling back to Base.
The what method of std::runtime_error returns the message string passed during construction, which is 'Error' in this case.

mcqs.question: What happens if a destructor throws an exception during exception handling?
mcqs.explanation: According to C++ standard, if a destructor invoked during stack unwinding throws an exception, std::terminate is called immediately as throwing from destructor during unwinding is considered a fatal error.

mcqs.question: Which catch block will catch a thrown integer value of 10?
mcqs.explanation: catch(int) is the only matching catch block for an integer exception. Other catch blocks are for different types and catch(...) would catch any type but is not needed here since catch(int) matches.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of catching exceptions by reference?

options.text: Prevents object slicing and allows polymorphism

mcqs.correctAnswer: Prevents object slicing and allows polymorphism
mcqs.explanation: Catching by reference prevents object slicing, which loses derived class information. It also allows polymorphic behavior through virtual functions and avoids unnecessary copy construction.
catch(...) must be the last catch block. Having catch(int) after catch(...) makes it unreachable, causing a compile error.

mcqs.question: What does the noexcept specifier guarantee?
mcqs.explanation: noexcept specifies that the function is not expected to throw. If it does throw, std::terminate is called. It serves as a promise to the compiler and enables certain optimizations.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a standard exception class?
mcqs.explanation: std::myException is not part of the C++ standard exception library. std::bad_alloc, std::runtime_error, and std::logic_error are all standard exception classes.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of rethrowing an exception?

options.text: To pass the exception to an outer handler

mcqs.correctAnswer: To pass the exception to an outer handler
mcqs.explanation: Rethrowing with 'throw;' passes the current exception to an outer try-catch block. This is useful when a catch block can partially handle the exception but needs to let outer handlers process it further.

mcqs.question: What is exception safety guarantee level where operation either succeeds or has no effect?
mcqs.explanation: The strong guarantee ensures that if an operation fails, the program state remains unchanged - it's either fully complete or effect (all or nothing).

mcqs.question: Which of the following is the base class for all standard C++ exceptions?
mcqs.explanation: std::exception is the base class for all standard library exceptions. Both std::runtime_error and std::logic_error are derived from std::exception.

mcqs.question: What happens during stack unwinding?

options.text: Destructors are called for local objects

mcqs.correctAnswer: Destructors are called for local objects
mcqs.explanation: Stack unwinding is the process of removing function stack frames and calling destructors for local objects as the exception propagates up the call stack, ensuring proper cleanup.

mcqs.question: Which catch block signature is recommended for catching derived class exceptions in a hierarchy?
mcqs.explanation: Catching by const reference is recommended because it prevents modification of the exception object, maintains polymorphism, avoids slicing, and works with both const and non-const exceptions.

E2 is-a E1, so when thrown, the first matching catch block (E1&) catches it. The E2& catch block is unreachable because E1& catches it first. This demonstrates why derived classes must be caught before base classes.

mcqs.question: Which of these is the correct hierarchy order from most general to most specific?
mcqs.explanation: std::exception is the base, with std::runtime_error derived from it. std::bad_alloc is derived from std::runtime_error (through std::bad_alloc -> std::exception in actual hierarchy).

mcqs.question: What is object slicing in the context of exception handling?

options.text: Derived class part is cut off when caught by value

mcqs.correctAnswer: Derived class part is cut off when caught by value
mcqs.explanation: Object slicing occurs when a derived class object is assigned to a base class object by value, losing all derived class-specific data and behavior. In exception handling, this happens when catching by value instead of by reference.

mcqs.question: Can constructors throw exceptions?

options.text: Yes, but must handle cleanup manually
Yes, and destructors of constructed members are called

mcqs.correctAnswer: Yes, and destructors of constructed members are called
mcqs.explanation: Constructors can throw exceptions. During stack unwinding, destructors of successfully constructed member objects and base class subobjects will be called, ensuring proper cleanup.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of try-catch blocks?
mcqs.explanation: Try-catch blocks provide a mechanism for handling runtime errors and exceptional conditions in a structured way, separating error handling code from normal program flow.

mcqs.question: Which keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception in C++?
mcqs.explanation: The throw keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception in C++. It can throw any type including primitive types, pointers, or class objects.
